target_node: ACS Committee on Trauma
evidence_locator: ACS Committee on Trauma 100th Anniversary (facs.org); ACS Scudder biography (facs.org/quality-programs/trauma/committee-on-trauma/scudder-oration-on-trauma/honoring-charles-locke-scudder)
notes: In 1922, Scudder organized a conference of 26 prominent surgeons at MGH to standardize fracture treatment. The ACS Board of Regents then established the Committee on Fractures with Scudder as first chair (1922-1933). The committee expanded to the Committee on Fractures and Other Trauma (1939), then was renamed the Committee on Trauma (1949). Today the ACS COT oversees 3,500+ Fellows, runs the trauma center verification program, ATLS, and TQIP (already in graph). Scudder also initiated the annual Oration on Fractures (1929), now the Scudder Oration on Trauma — one of the highest honors in trauma surgery.

target_node: William Altemeier
evidence_locator: University of Cincinnati Department of Surgery Mission/History (med.uc.edu/depart/surgery/about/mission-history); William A. Altemeier Collection (sites.libraries.uc.edu/altemier)
notes: Altemeier earned his MD from the University of Cincinnati in 1933 and studied surgery under Mont Reid (already in graph as Halsted trainee and UC Cincinnati department founder). Reid was chairman 1931-1943; Altemeier interned at Cincinnati General Hospital before completing residency at Henry Ford Hospital and a Master of Surgery at the University of Michigan. He returned to UC as Instructor in Surgery in 1940 and became the fourth Christian R. Holmes Professor and Chairman of Surgery in 1952, serving for 26 years. The Cincinnati departmental succession (Heuer → Reid → Carter → Altemeier) was known as the 'Hopkins Invasion.'

target_node: American Association for the Surgery of Trauma
notes: Scudder organized the founding of the AAST to elevate the study and systematic management of traumatic injuries.

target_node: Baltimore City Hospital Intensive Care Unit
notes: Safar established the first multi-disciplinary ICU in the United States, transitioning resuscitative science into a dedicated geographic hospital unit.

target_node: University of Maryland Shock Trauma Center
notes: Cowley operationalized the 'Golden Hour' concept, building the first fully integrated, standalone trauma hospital.

target_node: Society of Critical Care Medicine
notes: Safar was a primary founder and the first president of the SCCM, establishing the governance node for intensive care.

target_node: Society of Critical Care Medicine
notes: Shoemaker co-founded the SCCM, cementing the role of surgical physiology and hemodynamic optimization within the new specialty.

target_node: Surgical Infection Society
evidence_locator: Surgical Infection Society Leadership page (sisna.org/about/leadership); SIS 33rd Presidential Address, PMC4063369
notes: The SIS was founded by 10 members on a Saturday in April 1980, after an American Surgical Association meeting. Altemeier served as the first president. The society grew from 119 charter members to over 550 members and publishes the journal Surgical Infections. Altemeier's Cincinnati bacteriology laboratory (founded 1940) and his studies on surgical infections, antibiotic therapy, tetanus, gas gangrene, and wound infections over 349 publications were the scientific foundation for the society. He also served as ASA president and ACS president (1978).

target_node: Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma
evidence_locator: EAST Official History (east.org/about-east/history); Champion et al, EAST Memories document
notes: Schwab was one of 10 founding board members of EAST at the kickoff meeting on October 19, 1986 at the New Orleans Hilton. He was Professor of Surgery at the University of Pennsylvania and chief of the Division of Traumatology, Surgical Critical Care, and Emergency Surgery at Penn — connecting EAST to the Penn institutional trunk (I.S. Ravdin's department, Priority 5). EAST was founded because established trauma societies (AAST, Western Trauma Association) were perceived as too exclusive for younger surgeons. The founding document explicitly notes Cowley (already in graph) as 'no longer in active practice' by the mid-1980s — EAST was the next generation.

source_node: Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma
target_node: American Association for the Surgery of Trauma
evidence_locator: Champion et al, EAST Memories document (east.org/content/documents/east__memories_revised.pdf)
notes: At the September 1987 AAST meeting in Montreal, EAST founder Kimball Maull was summoned to a special meeting of the AAST Board of Managers. The AAST endorsed EAST as a 'wonderful concept' for encouraging young trauma surgeons, but only on condition that EAST collaborate with the AAST's Journal of Trauma rather than launch an independent journal. EAST accepted, gaining immediate credibility. This relationship parallels the Harvey Cushing Society's uneasy coexistence with the Society of Neurological Surgeons. Edge type 'programmatic_accreditation' captures the institutional endorsement relationship.

target_node: Penn Trauma and Surgical Critical Care Fellowship
notes: Schwab CW, Reilly PM, Haut E, Gracias VH, Kauder DR. 'Training in trauma surgery: quantitative and qualitative aspects of a new paradigm for fellowship.' Ann Surg. 2003 Oct;238(4):596-604. Schwab's own article documenting the design and outcomes of the Penn trauma fellowship he founded in 1989. By 2003, the program had trained dozens of fellows. As of 2024, over 100 fellows have completed the program and 'are now practicing around the world,' including 'Deans, Chief Executive Officers, Chairs of Surgery' per Penn's current fellowship website. Schwab (b. ~1944) was a Navy surgeon during Vietnam, completed residency at National Naval Medical Center, then became the founding Chief of the Division of Traumatology, Surgical Critical Care, and Emergency Surgery at Penn (1987). He is also co-author of the landmark 'Damage Control' paper (J Trauma 1993, with Rotondo). Schwab goes from degree 1 (EAST founder only) to degree 2, gaining an institutional anchor at Penn that connects the trauma module to the University of Pennsylvania — an existing graph institution (Ravdin→Penn already in graph).

target_node: Surgical Infection Prevention Project
notes: The SIS, along with the CDC and CMS, provided the scientific evidence base and clinical practice guidelines underpinning the Surgical Infection Prevention Project (2002) and its successor SCIP (2006). SIS members authored the foundational studies on antibiotic prophylaxis timing, selection, and duration that became SIP/SCIP process measures. The ACS Manual on Control of Infection in Surgical Patients, which Altemeier co-edited, was a direct precursor. Edge type 'programmatic_accreditation' captures the scientific endorsement relationship between the academic society and the federal quality initiative.

target_node: American Association for the Surgery of Trauma
notes: AAST Past Presidents list documents Schwab as AAST president (2004-2005). He served as president during the critical period when the AAST was developing the Acute Care Surgery fellowship paradigm (2003 joint committee formation → 2005 ACS Committee creation → 2008 first fellowship approval). This edge connects Schwab's EAST founding role (1986, already in graph) to the AAST governance hub, bridging the two major US trauma societies through a single person. Schwab is also past president of EAST (already in graph as society_founder) and past president of the International Association for Trauma Surgery and Intensive Care. Penn Trauma website and ACS 2024 award confirm his AAST presidency. No PMID found for a published AAST presidential address — AAST addresses are published in J Trauma and Acute Care Surgery but Schwab's specific address PMID could not be confirmed.

target_node: Surgical Care Improvement Project
notes: The narrow focus on infection was succeeded by SCIP to broadly mandate and measure perioperative metrics tied directly to CMS reimbursement.

target_node: Trauma Quality Improvement Program
notes: The ACS established TQIP to extend the NSQIP risk-adjusted modeling specifically into the trauma center verification process.

target_node: Trauma Quality Improvement Program
notes: The ACS COT developed TQIP as the trauma-specific quality improvement program paralleling ACS-NSQIP (already in graph). TQIP uses the National Trauma Data Bank for benchmarking and risk-adjusted outcomes. This edge clarifies that TQIP was a COT initiative, not a direct ACS general creation — the graph previously had ACS → TQIP (institutional_founder) which remains valid as the parent, but the COT was the operational creator.

source_node: American Association for the Surgery of Trauma
target_node: Acute Care Surgery Fellowship Program
notes: Davis KA, Jurkovich GJ. 'Fellowship training in Acute Care Surgery: from inception to current state.' Trauma Surg Acute Care Open. 2017 May 22;2(1):e000095. PMC5891699. In 2005, the AAST created the Acute Care Surgery Committee to develop a training paradigm combining trauma surgery, surgical critical care, and emergency general surgery. In 2008, the first ACS Fellowship was approved by the AAST Board of Managers. By 2017, 20 programs had been approved and 82 fellows trained. The fellowship is AAST-accredited (not ACGME) with a verification process modeled on trauma center verification. This edge captures the most significant fellowship formalization event in the trauma module — it transforms the AAST from a society with only founding/accreditation edges into a programmatic accreditor for post-residency training, paralleling the ABS→ABMS and ACGME accreditation pathways already in the graph. The Acute Care Surgery Fellowship Program is a new institutional node representing the national program collectively, not a single site.

target_node: University of Maryland Shock Trauma Center
evidence_locator: University of Maryland Medical Center Archives
notes: Shock Trauma integrated TQIP benchmarks, overlaying modern multi-center data science onto Cowley's foundational clinical framework.

target_node: Ben Taub General Hospital Trauma Center
evidence_locator: Baylor College of Medicine Department of Surgery Faculty Profile; bcm.edu/people-search/kenneth-mattox-26215
notes: Kenneth L. Mattox (b. 1938) served as chief of staff and surgeon-in-chief at Ben Taub General Hospital from 1973 to 2020, a 47-year tenure. He developed the internationally renowned Ben Taub Hospital Emergency Center and Trauma Center, building it into one of the busiest and highest-performing Level I trauma centers in the United States. He is Distinguished Service Professor in the Michael E. DeBakey Department of Surgery at Baylor College of Medicine. He authored over 600 articles and is co-editor of both the Mattox Trauma textbook (8th edition) and Sabiston's Textbook of Surgery. The DeBakey→Mattox training edge exists in Session 1 file (T2-01). This edge adds his institutional founding contribution. Ben Taub is a new institutional node representing the Houston public hospital trauma system. PMID upgrade candidate: search for Mattox tribute or profile in J Trauma or Am Surg.

target_node: American Association for the Surgery of Trauma
evidence_locator: Baylor College of Medicine Department of Surgery Faculty Profile; bcm.edu/people-search/kenneth-mattox-26215; multiple corroborating sources including ACS, AMA records
notes: Mattox is past president of the American Association for the Surgery of Trauma (AAST). Exact presidential term approximated as c. 1992-1993 based on career timeline; to be verified. He also served on the ACS Board of Governors, was ACS Second Vice-President-Elect, and received the 2013 AMA Benjamin Rush Award for Citizenship. This edge connects the DeBakey→Mattox→AAST chain, linking the Baylor cardiothoracic trunk to the trauma governance backbone. AAST is already in the graph with 4 edges.
